SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper backs its main empirical claims with multiple experiments across architectures (NIN, VGG, LSTM) and optimizers and uses interpolation visualizations and an output-distance metric to show different algorithms land in different solutions (Sections 3–4). However, several methodological details are missing or weakly addressed: the number of random seeds/runs and statistical significance tests are not reported, the identification of ‘‘local minima’’ relies on approximate zero gradients along 1-D projections without Hessian/eigenvalue checks, and some causal statements (e.g. batch-norm ‘‘redeems’’ ill-posed surfaces) are not fully isolated from confounders. Overall the experiments are suggestive and mostly coherent but leave gaps that limit the strength of the technical conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The work extends prior visualization-based analyses (Goodfellow et al.) by comparing multiple popular optimizers, introducing 2-D barycentric/bilinear interpolations, studying late-stage optimizer switching, and experimenting with RK2 augmentations — these are useful empirical extensions. Nevertheless the central theme (empirical study of loss surfaces) is a well-explored problem and the contributions are largely incremental rather than introducing fundamentally new methodology or theory.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well structured (intro, related work, experiments, conclusions) and most methods and hyperparameters are described in the Technical Details and Appendix. Yet the manuscript has notable presentation issues in the provided text (repeated/duplicated paragraphs in A.6, some informal phrasing, and several places where figures/tables are referenced but not included here), and certain experimental choices (exact number of runs, full tuning protocol) are not communicated clearly, which hampers reproducibility.
